personality_voice.book_baseline: Ron is loyal, warm, impulsive, and quick to defend those he cares about. He is funny in a dry, reactive way - his humor comes from bluntness and exasperation rather than performance. He is insecure about his family's poverty and about living in the shadow of his brothers, and this insecurity surfaces in self-deprecating remarks and embarrassed deflections (ears going pink, cutting himself off mid-sentence about not being able to afford an owl). He is brave but not reckless - he volunteers as a second in the duel, sacrifices himself at chess, and goes back for Hermione despite not wanting to. His speech is casual, direct, and often blunt: 'Shut up,' 'She needs to sort out her priorities,' 'Don't be an idiot.' He reacts to Hermione with irritation and dismissiveness early on, and to Malfoy with hot-headed anger. He is not a leader but he is not passive - he initiates, advises, and acts.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves Ron's warmth, loyalty, bluntness, and reactive humor. His line 'She needs to sort out her priorities' is a direct lift from the book's spirit. His exasperation with Hermione ('You're saying it wrong' / 'You do it then'), his hot-headed lunge at Malfoy, his casual bravery at chess, and his embarrassed deflections (holding up mushed sandwiches instead of buying from the trolley) are all present. His speech patterns - short, direct, often grumpy - are preserved. The 'Wicked!' reaction to Harry's scar and 'That's wizard's chess' are tonally accurate.
personality_voice.difference: The film removes the extended train conversation in which Ron's insecurity about his brothers is explicitly articulated ('Everyone expects me to do as well as the others, but if I do, it's no big deal, because they did it first'). The Mirror of Erised scene is present in the film and preserves the core insight - Ron sees himself as head boy and Quidditch captain - but the book's version is richer in Ron's reaction (he tears his eyes away 'excitedly,' wants more time at the mirror, and briefly argues with Harry about it). The film Ron is slightly more reactive and less verbally textured than the book Ron, but the dominant traits are intact. The film also omits Ron's running commentary during the Quidditch match (defending Neville, snapping at Malfoy, eventually physically attacking him), which shows his protectiveness and temper - though the physical attack on Malfoy is retained.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main personality traits - loyalty, bluntness, insecurity, reactive humor, impulsive protectiveness - are preserved with enough specificity that the character is recognizable and substantially faithful. The loss of the extended train dialogue removes the most explicit articulation of his insecurity about his brothers, which is a secondary but meaningful trait. This warrants a minor deduction but not a heavy one, as the Mirror of Erised scene compensates by showing the same insecurity visually and through Dumbledore's narration.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Ron serves multiple narrative functions in Book 1: he is Harry's first friend and social guide to the wizarding world (train scene), a co-investigator in the Flamel/Stone mystery, the tactical mind behind the chess chamber, and an emotional anchor. He initiates actions - he volunteers as Harry's second in the duel, he suggests sending Norbert to Charlie, he leads the chess strategy, he physically attacks Malfoy to defend Harry and Neville. He is the one who identifies the Norwegian Ridgeback, who warns Harry about Snape's behavior, who keeps watch during the mirror visits. His agency is consistent and meaningful throughout.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Ron's most important act of agency: the chess sacrifice. He also retains his role as social guide (explaining Chocolate Frogs, wizard chess, Quidditch), co-investigator (identifying the Ridgeback, finding the Flamel book section on dragons), and loyal defender (attacking Malfoy). He correctly identifies the Devil's Snare situation as requiring Hermione's knowledge ('Now what are we gonna do?!') and contributes to the key room by spotting the rusty key.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several acts of agency are reduced or transferred. In the book, it is Ron who suggests sending Norbert to Charlie - a meaningful plot-driving idea. In the film this is compressed and the suggestion is less clearly attributed. In the book, Ron physically attacks Malfoy during the Quidditch match and is described as 'on top of him, wrestling him to the ground' while also defending Neville verbally ('You tell him, Neville') - the film retains the physical attack but removes the verbal defense of Neville. More significantly, in the book Ron is the one who warns Harry not to push Snape ('Don't push it, I've heard Snape can turn very nasty') and who actively theorizes about Snape throughout - in the film, Harry takes more of the investigative lead and Hermione provides more of the analytical framing. Ron's role in the Norbert subplot (being bitten, going to the hospital wing, the letter being stolen by Malfoy) is entirely absent from the film, removing a sequence where Ron bears real consequences for his loyalty.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The chess sacrifice is preserved and is Ron's most important single act of agency. However, the Norbert subplot removal eliminates a sequence where Ron's loyalty has real cost (dragon bite, hospital wing, Malfoy's leverage). The investigative agency is somewhat redistributed toward Harry and Hermione. These are meaningful reductions but the core function - loyal friend, tactical contributor, brave self-sacrificer - remains. The score reflects a reduction but not a wholesale reassignment.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Ron's primary motivations are loyalty to Harry and a desire to prove himself - both to his family and to himself. His insecurity about being overshadowed by his brothers is explicitly stated on the train and confirmed by the Mirror of Erised. He is also motivated by a genuine love of the wizarding world (Quidditch, wizard chess, magical creatures) that makes him an enthusiastic participant rather than a reluctant one. His internal conflict is mild but real: he wants to be brave and capable but is aware of his limitations; he is embarrassed by his family's poverty but defensive about it.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The Mirror of Erised scene preserves the core motivation - Ron sees himself as head boy and Quidditch captain, confirming that his deepest desire is to be recognized as exceptional in his own right. His loyalty to Harry is consistently shown. His embarrassment about poverty is shown through the mushed sandwiches on the train. His love of the wizarding world is shown through his enthusiasm about Quidditch and wizard chess.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The explicit articulation of the 'living in brothers' shadows' motivation is absent from the film - it is only implied through the Mirror scene. The book gives Ron a full speech about it on the train, which establishes it as a conscious, named anxiety rather than something the audience must infer. The film also removes the moment where Ron's ears go pink when he nearly mentions his family can't afford an owl - a small but precise behavioral signal of his shame. These are simplifications rather than changes, but they flatten the motivation from something Ron is consciously aware of to something the audience must read into the mirror scene.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are simplified but not changed. The Mirror of Erised scene is the most important evidence and it is preserved. The loss of the explicit train speech means the motivation is less foregrounded, but it is not absent or replaced. A moderate deduction is appropriate.

character_arc.book_baseline: Ron's arc in Book 1 is modest but real: he begins as a slightly insecure, somewhat dismissive boy who is awed by Harry's fame and irritated by Hermione's competence. Over the course of the year he becomes a genuine friend to both, overcomes his dismissiveness of Hermione (acknowledging 'Good of her to get us out of trouble like that'), and demonstrates real courage and self-sacrifice at the chess board. The arc ends with him receiving recognition from Dumbledore for 'the best-played game of chess Hogwarts has seen in many years' - a moment that directly addresses his need to be seen as exceptional.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the key beats: Ron begins awed by Harry, warms to Hermione after the troll incident, and sacrifices himself at chess. The Dumbledore recognition scene is present. The arc is compressed but the shape is intact.
character_arc.difference: The film compresses the Hermione reconciliation. In the book, there is a gradual thaw - Ron admits she was 'good of her' to cover for them, and the narration notes they became friends after the troll incident. In the film the reconciliation is faster and less textured. The book also shows Ron's arc through smaller moments - defending Neville, standing by Harry when the whole school turns against him after the points loss - that are absent from the film. The points-loss sequence (where 'only Ron stood by him') is entirely cut, removing a moment that shows Ron's loyalty at its most costly and his arc at a meaningful point.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but not falsified. The chess sacrifice and Dumbledore's recognition are the climactic arc moments and both are present. The loss of the 'only Ron stood by him' sequence removes a meaningful arc beat. The score reflects compression with one important step missing.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Ron's two key relationships in Book 1 are with Harry (immediate friendship, mutual curiosity, loyalty) and with Hermione (initial irritation and dismissiveness that evolves into friendship after the troll). His relationship with his family - particularly the weight of his brothers' achievements - is also important but mostly expressed through internal anxiety rather than direct interaction. His relationship with Malfoy is one of immediate mutual hostility, with Ron defending Harry and his own family's honor.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry-Ron friendship is well-preserved: the train scene establishes mutual warmth, the shared adventures build it, and the chess sacrifice is its emotional peak. The Ron-Hermione dynamic is preserved in its arc shape - irritation to friendship - though compressed. The Ron-Malfoy hostility is present (the snicker at Malfoy's name, the physical attack during Quidditch).
key_relationships.difference: The Ron-family dynamic is significantly reduced. The book's train scene gives Ron a full articulation of his relationship to his brothers' legacy, and the Christmas sequence shows Ron's family warmth (Mrs. Weasley's sweater, Fred and George's teasing). The film retains the sweater moment but removes the extended family texture. The Ron-Hermione relationship loses some of its book texture: the book shows Ron being genuinely uncomfortable after making Hermione cry ('he looked a bit uncomfortable'), which is a small but important signal that his dismissiveness is not cruelty. The film's Ron mimics Hermione in the corridor but the discomfort beat is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The two primary relationships (Harry, Hermione) are preserved in dynamic and function. The family relationship is reduced but not falsified. The Ron-Hermione dynamic loses some nuance. These are compressions rather than distortions, warranting a moderate deduction.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Ron's complexity in Book 1 comes from several sources: his insecurity coexisting with genuine confidence in areas he knows well (chess, Quidditch knowledge, wizarding world lore); his dismissiveness of Hermione coexisting with real discomfort when he hurts her; his bravery coexisting with fear (pale under his freckles before the Sorting, 'This is the end!' when locked out); his loyalty coexisting with self-interest (wanting more time at the mirror). The Norbert subplot shows Ron bearing real physical consequences for loyalty (dragon bite, hospital wing). The post-points-loss sequence shows Ron as the only person who stands by Harry when it is socially costly. The Mirror of Erised scene in the book is richer - Ron wants more time at the mirror, briefly argues with Harry, and is pulled away reluctantly.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves Ron's chess expertise as a domain of genuine competence, his fear-bravery coexistence (visible during the chess game), and his loyalty. The Mirror scene preserves the core insight. The troll scene preserves his reactive bravery.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The Norbert subplot is entirely absent, removing the dragon bite, hospital wing scene, and Malfoy's leverage - a sequence that shows Ron's loyalty having real physical cost and his vulnerability to Malfoy's cruelty. The post-points-loss sequence ('only Ron stood by him') is absent, removing Ron's most costly act of loyalty. The extended Mirror of Erised scene is compressed, losing Ron's reluctance to leave and his brief argument with Harry. The train scene's explicit insecurity speech is absent. The moment where Ron is uncomfortable after making Hermione cry is absent. Cumulatively, the film preserves the broad strokes of Ron's complexity but removes several of the specific scenes that give it texture and weight.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The removed material - Norbert subplot, post-points-loss loyalty, extended mirror scene, train insecurity speech - collectively represents a meaningful reduction in the character's complexity. None of these individually would be disqualifying, but together they flatten Ron from a character with visible internal contradictions and costly loyalties to a simpler 'loyal best friend' function. The score reflects noticeable simplification without complete flattening.

lost_or_transferred_material: Ron's explicit train speech about living in his brothers' shadows ('Everyone expects me to do as well as the others, but if I do, it's no big deal, because they did it first')
Ron's suggestion to send Norbert to Charlie - a plot-driving idea that is his in the book
The entire Norbert subplot: Ron being bitten by the dragon, hospitalized, Malfoy stealing Charlie's letter from Ron's book
The post-points-loss sequence where 'only Ron stood by him' when the whole school turned against Harry
Ron's discomfort after making Hermione cry ('he looked a bit uncomfortable')
Ron's extended Mirror of Erised scene - reluctance to leave, brief argument with Harry about whose turn it is
Ron warning Harry not to push Snape in Potions ('Don't push it, I've heard Snape can turn very nasty')
Ron defending Neville verbally during the Quidditch match ('You tell him, Neville')
Ron's ears going pink when he nearly mentions his family can't afford an owl - behavioral signal of shame
Ron's running Quidditch commentary defending Harry and snapping at Malfoy in the stands
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or act of agency is transferred or removed): The Norbert subplot removal eliminates a sequence where Ron's agency and loyalty have real cost; the 'only Ron stood by him' sequence is absent. These together constitute meaningful agency and narrative function loss. Score kept at 73, below the 75 cap threshold.
key_observations: The film's Ron Weasley is substantially faithful in personality, voice, and the shape of his arc - the chess sacrifice, the Mirror of Erised scene, the troll incident, and the train friendship are all present and tonally accurate. The dominant traits (loyalty, bluntness, reactive humor, insecurity about recognition) are preserved. However, the film removes or compresses several scenes that give Ron's book characterization its texture: the explicit insecurity speech on the train, the Norbert subplot with its real physical consequences, the post-points-loss loyalty sequence, and the extended mirror scene. These losses collectively simplify Ron from a character with visible internal contradictions and costly loyalties into a somewhat flatter 'loyal best friend' function. The film Ron is recognizable and largely faithful, but the removed material meaningfully reduces his complexity without replacement.
